It is owned by [Radio One](/wiki/Radio_One_(Company) "Radio One (Company)") and airs an [urban adult contemporary](/wiki/Urban_adult_contemporary "Urban adult contemporary") [radio format](/wiki/Radio_format "Radio format"). WRNB's studios and offices are in the Two Bala Plaza– building on City Line Avenue in [Bala Cynwyd](/wiki/Bala_Cynwyd "Bala Cynwyd").

**WRNB** (100.3 [FM](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting")) is a [commercial radio](/wiki/Commercial_radio "Commercial radio") station [licensed](/wiki/City_of_license "City of license") to [Media, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Media,_Pennsylvania "Media, Pennsylvania") in the [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")–[Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley") [radio market](/wiki/Radio_market "Radio market"). It is owned by [Radio One](/wiki/Radio_One_(Company) "Radio One (Company)") and airs an [urban adult contemporary](/wiki/Urban_adult_contemporary "Urban adult contemporary") [radio format](/wiki/Radio_format "Radio format"). In afternoon [drive time](/wiki/Drive_time "Drive time"), WRNB carries the [syndicated](/wiki/Radio_syndication "Radio syndication") [D. L. Hughley](/wiki/D._L._Hughley "D. L. Hughley") Show. WRNB's studios and offices are in the Two Bala Plaza– building on City Line Avenue in [Bala Cynwyd](/wiki/Bala_Cynwyd "Bala Cynwyd").

WRNB has an [effective radiated power](/wiki/Effective_radiated_power "Effective radiated power") (ERP) of 17,000 watts. The [transmitter](/wiki/Transmitter "Transmitter") is located in the [Roxborough](/wiki/Roxborough,_Philadelphia "Roxborough, Philadelphia") neighborhood of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") at ([40°2′36.0″N 75°14′32.0″W﻿ / ﻿40.043333°N 75.242222°W﻿ / 40.043333; -75.242222](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=WRNB&params=40_2_36.0_N_75_14_32.0_W_)).[[3]](#cite_note-3) The station is short-spaced with two other co-channel FM stations (see [See § Signal note](#Signal_note) below). WRNB broadcasts in the [HD Radio](/wiki/HD_Radio "HD Radio") hybrid format, with its HD-2 subchannel airing the [urban contemporary](/wiki/Urban_contemporary "Urban contemporary") format that moved from its main signal, and the HD-3 subchannel airing an [urban contemporary gospel](/wiki/Urban_contemporary_gospel "Urban contemporary gospel") format.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Prior stations on 100.3 FM

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prior stations on 100.3 FM")]

In 1942, a station broadcasting on 100.3 FM first [signed on](/wiki/Signed_on "Signed on") as KYW-FM, which was licensed to Philadelphia. It was the [sister station](/wiki/Sister_station "Sister station") to [KYW](/wiki/KYW_(AM) "KYW (AM)"), owned by the [Westinghouse Electric Corporation](/wiki/Westinghouse_Electric_Corporation "Westinghouse Electric Corporation"). By the late 1940s, KYW-FM moved to 92.5 MHz.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The 100.3 frequency remained [dark](/wiki/Dark_(broadcasting) "Dark (broadcasting)") for a decade and a half.

In 1962, WXUR-FM began broadcasting, licensed to the suburban community of Media.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Its effective radiated power was 4,200 watts, [simulcasting](/wiki/Simulcast "Simulcast") the [Christian programming](/wiki/Christian_radio "Christian radio") of its AM sister station 690 WXUR (now [WPHE](/wiki/WPHE "WPHE") in [Phoenixville, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Phoenixville,_Pennsylvania "Phoenixville, Pennsylvania")). WXUR-AM-FM were owned by [Carl McIntire](/wiki/Carl_McIntire "Carl McIntire"), a [Bible Presbyterian](/wiki/Bible_Presbyterian_Church "Bible Presbyterian Church") minister.

In 1973, the [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") (FCC) revoked the licenses for both stations, with the FCC ruling that WXUR-AM-FM violated the [Fairness Doctrine](/wiki/Fairness_Doctrine "Fairness Doctrine"), refusing to air competing viewpoints. The 100.3 frequency then went silent again for eight years.

### 1981-1993: Adult contemporary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1981-1993: Adult contemporary")]

In 1981, after a seven-year [comparative hearing](/wiki/Comparative_hearing "Comparative hearing"), the FCC awarded the license to the Greater Media Radio Company, owned by Daniel Lerner. The name referred to the station's city of license, [Media](/wiki/Media,_Pennsylvania "Media, Pennsylvania"), and was not associated with the larger [Greater Media](/wiki/Greater_Media "Greater Media"), Inc., which locally owned [WMGK](/wiki/WMGK "WMGK"), [WPEN](/wiki/WKDN_(AM) "WKDN (AM)") and other stations around the U.S. On November 8, 1982, 100.3 FM returned to the air, this time as WKSZ, "Kiss 100," with a [soft adult contemporary](/wiki/Soft_adult_contemporary "Soft adult contemporary") format.[[6]](#cite_note-6) By 1987, Kiss 100 was the No. 1 [Arbitron](/wiki/Arbitron "Arbitron")-ranked station among women ages 25 to 54.

In the early 1990s, however, the battle for AC listeners heated up, with WEAZ (now [WBEB](/wiki/WBEB "WBEB")) becoming the dominant station for soft music in Philadelphia. WKSZ lost ground in the ratings, falling to 17th place in 1992. To improve ratings, WKSZ tried to combine AC and [oldies](/wiki/Oldies "Oldies") with what the station called the "50/50 Mix"; however, this failed to turn ratings around, and in 1993, WKSZ returned to a [playlist](/wiki/Playlist "Playlist") of AC love songs.

### 1993-2005: Alternative rock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1993-2005: Alternative rock")]

On March 15, 1993, at 6 a.m., with the station still struggling, WKSZ became "Z100", switching to an [adult top 40](/wiki/Adult_top_40 "Adult top 40") format. The flip came three days after [Eagle 106](/wiki/WUMR_(FM) "WUMR (FM)") gave up Top 40 and switched to [smooth jazz](/wiki/Smooth_jazz "Smooth jazz"), creating an opening which WKSZ hoped to fill.[[7]](#cite_note-7) New York's [WHTZ](/wiki/WHTZ "WHTZ"), located on the same frequency of 100.3 MHz, and calling itself "Z100" since 1983, demanded that WKSZ drop the name to avoid listener confusion. After a brief legal battle, the call sign and name were changed to WPLY, "Y100".

The station initially had a slight [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") lean, but still played other Top 40 hits, including pop and dance. By early 1995, WPLY evolved into a full-time [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") format, which lasted nearly ten years.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) Through the late 1990s, WPLY leaned towards [modern AC](/wiki/Modern_AC "Modern AC"), and would shift towards an [active rock](/wiki/Active_rock "Active rock") lean in 2000.

In 2000, the station was bought by [Radio One](/wiki/Radio_One_(Company) "Radio One (Company)") for $80 million.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Radio One focused mostly on urban music and did not plan to program an alternative rock station. The company tried to make a deal to swap formats with Greater Media's WEJM (now [WBEN-FM](/wiki/WBEN-FM "WBEN-FM")), which at the time, ran a [rhythmic oldies](/wiki/Rhythmic_oldies "Rhythmic oldies") format called "Jammin Gold", which would have better fit into Radio One's portfolio. The deal fell through, and Radio One continued to run WPLY as an alternative rock station for nearly five years. While ratings had gone down, the station still was moderately successful.

### 2005-2011: Urban contemporary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2005-2011: Urban contemporary")]

On February 24, [2005](/wiki/2005_in_radio "2005 in radio"), at 11:50 pm, WPLY ended its alternative rock format with "[Alive](/wiki/Alive_(Pearl_Jam_song) "Alive (Pearl Jam song)")" by [Pearl Jam](/wiki/Pearl_Jam "Pearl Jam") (which was also the first and last song on [WDRE](/wiki/WPHI-FM "WPHI-FM")).[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) WPHI, an [urban contemporary](/wiki/Urban_contemporary "Urban contemporary") station at 103.9 MHz, also owned by Radio One and known as "103.9 The Beat," moved to 100.3 FM. The station at 103.9, in turn, flipped to [urban gospel](/wiki/Urban_gospel "Urban gospel") as [WPPZ-FM](/wiki/WPHI "WPHI"), "Praise 103.9".[[13]](#cite_note-13) By [2006](/wiki/2006_in_radio "2006 in radio"), [Nielsen BDS](/wiki/Nielsen_BDS "Nielsen BDS")/[Radio & Records](/wiki/Radio_%26_Records "Radio & Records") moved WPHI to the [urban contemporary](/wiki/Urban_contemporary "Urban contemporary") panel. [Mediabase](/wiki/Mediabase "Mediabase") followed suit in [2011](/wiki/2011_in_radio "2011 in radio"). WPHI-FM's chief rival was [WUSL](/wiki/WUSL "WUSL"), which had been Philadelphia's long-time Urban Contemporary leader.

In fall 2005, former rival personality Colby Colb was hired as the Program Director and afternoon host. For the next few years, 100.3 The Beat enjoyed high ratings, helped by Miss Jones in the morning and Colby Colb in the afternoon. Monie Love and Pooch took over mornings in 2005, with other disc jockeys on the station including Micheal Shawn, DJ Touchtone, DJ Jay Ski, Megatron, DJ Bent Rock, Moshay, Toshamakia, DJ JDS, Laiya and Hansoul. In late 2010, WPHI-FM became the Philadelphia home of the Star and Buc Wild Morning Show, [syndicated](/wiki/Radio_syndication "Radio syndication") from New York City. The show had actually been heard in Philadelphia in early 2006 on WUSL but had drawn some controversy and was dropped.

### 2011-2013: R&B

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2011-2013: R&B")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/100.3_WRNB.png/250px-100.3_WRNB.png)](/wiki/File:100.3_WRNB.png)

Logo as "100.3 WRNB" from 2011 to 2013

On August 27, [2011](/wiki/2011_in_radio "2011 in radio"), WPHI dropped the "Beat" moniker for "100.3 Philly." Radio One was preparing to make another frequency switch, this time moving WRNB's [urban AC](/wiki/Urban_AC "Urban AC") format and branding from 107.9 MHz to 100.3 MHz on September 1.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) WRNB was simulcast on both 100.3 and 107.9 until September 2, when the WPHI call sign moved to 107.9, adopted the former "Beat" format and rebranded as "Hot 107.9."

### 2013-2016: Classic R&B

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2013-2016: Classic R&B")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Oldschool1003.png/250px-Oldschool1003.png)](/wiki/File:Oldschool1003.png)

Logo as "Old School 100.3" from 2013 to 2016

On March 29, 2013, WRNB went jockless and began promoting a "big announcement" at 5 p.m. on April 1. At that time, after playing "[Keep Your Head to the Sky](/wiki/Keep_Your_Head_to_the_Sky "Keep Your Head to the Sky")" by [Earth, Wind, & Fire](/wiki/Earth,_Wind,_%26_Fire "Earth, Wind, & Fire"), WRNB flipped to [urban oldies](/wiki/Urban_oldies "Urban oldies") as "Old School 100.3." The first song on "Old School" was "[Atomic Dog](/wiki/Atomic_Dog "Atomic Dog")" by [George Clinton](/wiki/George_Clinton_(funk_musician) "George Clinton (funk musician)").[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The format played [Motown](/wiki/Motown "Motown"), [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco"), [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), [new jack swing](/wiki/New_jack_swing "New jack swing"), [freestyle](/wiki/Freestyle_music "Freestyle music"), and early [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop "Hip hop") from the 1960s to the early 2000s. The new format was designed to better compete with the dominant Urban AC station in Philadelphia, [WDAS-FM](/wiki/WDAS-FM "WDAS-FM").

In November 2014, sister station [WPHI-FM](/wiki/WPHI-FM "WPHI-FM") moved to a [classic hip-hop](/wiki/Classic_hip-hop "Classic hip-hop") format. In response, WRNB re-added some current music, although the station continued to focus on older urban hits.

### 2016-2020: R&B

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2016-2020: R&B")]

On October 6, 2016, WRNB returned to [urban adult contemporary](/wiki/Urban_adult_contemporary "Urban adult contemporary") music, once more calling itself "100.3 WRNB," and dropping the "Old School" branding.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/WRNB.png/250px-WRNB.png)](/wiki/File:WRNB.png)

Logo as "100.3 WRNB" from 2016 to 2019

### 2020-2022: Urban contemporary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2020-2022: Urban contemporary")]

On November 16, 2020, at 10 am, WRNB absorbed sister station WPHI's urban contemporary format into its own and was rebranded as "100.3 R&B & Hip Hop" (subsequently, WRNB's urban adult contemporary format would move full-time to its HD2 sub-channel). This was a result of Urban One selling said station to Entercom; the two stations simulcasted for a week, as it had been previously announced that WPHI would begin simulcasting [KYW](/wiki/KYW_(AM) "KYW (AM)") ahead of the sale's closure, on November 23.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### 2022–present: R&B

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2022–present: R&B")]

With the combined R&B/hip hop format failing to catch on in the market - bottoming out with a 1.0 share in the July 2022 Nielsen Audio ratings (well behind WUSL's 3.6 share), the station ultimately reverted to its previous urban AC format and "100.3 RNB" branding on August 25, 2022. With the shift, Program Director/afternoon host Paris Nicole exited the station after twelve years; Nicole previously served in the same position at WPHI and moved over at WRNB with the previous format shift.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Signal note

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Signal note")]

WRNB is short-spaced to two other co-channel Class B stations: [WHTZ](/wiki/WHTZ "WHTZ") *Z100* (licensed to serve [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey")) and [WBIG-FM](/wiki/WBIG-FM "WBIG-FM") *Big 100* (licensed to serve Washington, D.C.). The cities WRNB and WHTZ are licensed to serve are only 86 miles apart,[[21]](#cite_note-21) while the cities WRNB and WBIG-FM are licensed to serve are only 112 miles apart.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The minimum distance between two Class B stations operating on the same channel according to current [FCC](/wiki/FCC "FCC") rules is 150 miles.[[23]](#cite_note-FCC-23)

## History of WRNB call sign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=WRNB&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: History of WRNB call sign")]

Beginning in the 1950s, the call sign WRNB was licensed to [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina")'s first full-time [rock 'n roll](/wiki/Rock_%27n_roll "Rock 'n roll") radio station, a 1000-watt outlet in [New Bern](/wiki/New_Bern "New Bern") located at [AM 1490](/wiki/AM_1490 "AM 1490") (now [sports radio](/wiki/Sports_radio "Sports radio") [WWNB](/wiki/WWNB "WWNB")). WRNB 1490 was also famous for being one of the first radio stations to play [Carolina Beach Music](/wiki/Carolina_Beach_Music "Carolina Beach Music").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
